author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(perceus): P0 pipeline-review items — oracle crash-skip, dual-position RC double-consumption, FBIP same_arity conflation

1. test/test_properties.ml oracle_check: a signal-killed compiled binary
   (exit >= 128) when the interpreter succeeded is now a differential
   FAILURE, not a skip; clean nonzero error exits (and timeout's 124)
   still skip. RC miscompiles that abort were invisible to the oracle.

2. lib/tir/perceus.ml EApp + ECallPtr-extern: a var passed at BOTH an
   owned and a borrowed position of the same call, dead afterwards, was
   consumed twice (owned transfer + borrowed post-call dec) against the
   caller's single reference — both(s, s, 1) with both(a:own, b:borrow, n)
   aborted exit 134 compiled, interpreter fine. Normal calls now add one
   balancing EIncRC and keep the post-dec (also protects the borrowed
   alias for the call's whole duration); self calls drop the post-dec
   (TCO makes the trailing dec dead code — an inc would leak per
   iteration).

3. lib/tir/perceus.ml same_arity: stop conflating a TCon's type-parameter
   count with a constructor's field count. The dead-binding FBIP path fed
   raw declared types, so a dead 1-field Small(n) : Holder(a,b,c,d,e)
   cell was reused for the 5-field Big ctor — heap overflow clobbering
   the neighbouring chunk (compiled match took the wrong arm).
   add_scrutinee_free_for's arity encoding now carries an unforgeable
   $fbip$ marker; same_arity accepts only marked encodings. Audited
   Borrow.has_matching_alloc: name-prefix ownership gate only, no arity
   use — documented, no change needed.

Tests: 2 compiled Slow regression guards (parity + exit 0) in
test_stdlib_suite.ml; fbip_p8 unit tests updated to the marker encoding
+ same_arity_raw_type_refused added. 372 compiler / 224 eval / 323
codegen / 790 stdlib / 53 stdlib_march / 36 properties pass; RC
benchmarks (tree_transform / list_ops / binary_trees) outputs and
timings unchanged.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(borrow): generalize non-escaping-closure capture rule (join points + __try_call*)

Extends fd520110: a free variable captured by a closure is BORROWED (not owned)
whenever the closure does not ESCAPE the current function — it is only invoked
locally (ECallPtr callee: join points minted by join_points, immediately-applied
lambdas) or consumed by __try_call/__try_call_val (which invoke-and-free the thunk
without retaining it). owned_in defers such a capture to the FV's remaining uses.

New closure_escapes helper: clo escapes iff returned, stored in an alloc/record,
or passed as a DATA argument to a call. NOT escaping: clo as an ECallPtr callee, or
as the thunk arg of __try_call* (which lower to ECallPtr(__try_call*, [thunk])).

Effect on Depot: the whole transaction/migration chain now borrows conn —
tx_begin/tx_commit/tx_rollback, Transaction.run(tx_conn:borrow),
Migration.run/migrate(conn:borrow). Previously tx_begin owned conn because the
join_points pass factors its shared 'Db.query(conn, …)' continuation into a
conn-capturing join-point closure, and the EAlloc capture wrongly marked conn owned.

Validated: 1706 tests green (run_compiler 372 / run_eval 224 / run_codegen 322 /
run_stdlib 788).

NOTE: the depot Connection.close@0x13 crash STILL reproduces — its remaining owner
is the test harness's higher-order 'body(conn)' / 'wcb(conn)' call (an opaque
closure param invoked via ECallPtr, conservatively owning conn). That is a separate
HOF/borrow-polymorphism layer.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(forge): print wall time, error count, and warning count after build/check

forge build and forge check now print a summary line at the end of every
run: wall-clock time in seconds, plus error/warning counts when non-zero.

Examples:
  0.23s
  3 warnings — 1.25s
  2 errors, 1 warning — 0.47s

Implemented by capturing each compiler invocation's stderr via a temp file
(re-emitting it to forge's stderr with colour preserved when the terminal
is a tty), then scanning the captured output for "-- ERROR" / "-- WARNING"
block headers and the simpler "file:col: error:" one-liner format.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
merge: integrate pattern guard path threading with refinement-verified state migration

Combines the guard-aware division-safety + multi-position return inference
from claude/happy-ritchie-81ea80 with the @invariant syntax, --check-migration
mode, cap pure/no_extern/deterministic, cap no_alloc, and let-value division
checks from origin/main (claude/unruffled-feistel-d310a9).

Merge strategy:
- division_safety.ml: keep both path_proves_nonzero (guard fast-path) and
  collect_let_values (let-binding SMT check); check_var_divisor takes both
  path and let_values; None case tries path first, then let_values lookup
- return_infer.ml: keep multi-position return_positions + infer_clause with
  guard-derived Z3 assumptions; infer_decls adapted to inferred_return option
- test_compiler.ml: all 5 guard divsafety tests + all cap_pure/no_alloc tests
  + 4 return_refine_guard tests; 370 compiler tests pass
- specs: both todos entries kept; progress updated to 2026-06-26

370 compiler / 66 refinecheck tests pass.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(refinecheck/gap3): --check-migration compiler mode (stub gap2 deps)

Add Gap #3 of the refinement-verified state migration feature:
a new --check-migration CLI mode that verifies migrate_state soundness
across actor versions via the existing check_fn_post SMT machinery.

Changes:
- lib/refinecheck/refine_check.ml: export register_types_for_check, which
  primes the ADT/record type tables (clearing stale state first) so that
  check_fn_post can reflect field projections in migration VCs.

- bin/main.ml: add parse_pred/schema_str_to_ty/make_rawrecord_decl/
  find_migrate_fn/patch_migrate_fn helpers; add --check-migration,
  --prior-schema, --new-schema CLI flags; add dispatch block in compile()
  that synthesises a refined migrate_state signature and runs check_fn_post.

- bin/dune: add march_forge library dependency for Schema_diff access.

- forge/lib/cmd_deploy_hot.ml: add migration VC shell-out block between
  the compat check and the ACTIVATE loop.

Both gap2 stubs are marked STUB-GAP2:
  - parse_pred returns None (no expr_eof parser entry point yet)
  - schema_invariant returns None (no invariant field in actor_schema yet)
  - needs_vc_check = false in cmd_deploy_hot.ml

With stubs in place, --check-migration is a no-op (all actors hit the
None early-exit) and existing tests are unaffected. Remove stubs after
Gap #2 merges (two-line change each).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(refinecheck): Z3 division-safety pass for cap no_panic modules

Division/modulo ops are removed from typecheck's syntactic panic-surface
ban and handed to a new `lib/refinecheck/division_safety.ml` pass that
can approve them when the divisor carries a {v : Int | v != 0} (or
v > 0, v >= 1, etc.) refinement.

Syntactic fast-path handles v > 0, v >= 1, v != 0, v < 0, and &&
conjunctions without Z3. For everything else in the linear-arithmetic
fragment, `Refine.discharge` is called. Soundness: Unverified (Z3
absent or timeout) raises an error, preserving the cap no_panic
guarantee. Literal zero → always error; literal non-zero → safe;
complex divisor expressions → conservative error.

327 compiler tests pass (+6 new divsafety cases in cap_no_panic suite);
eval 224 / codegen 320 unchanged.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(refinecheck): EField/TDRecord record type refinements (Gap #1 spike)

Extends the post-typecheck refinement pass to discharge predicates over
record-typed return values:

- Register TDRecord types as 1-ctor SMT datatypes (sort name M_<Name>,
  ctor name = type name, selectors <Ctor>_<idx>)
- New ctor_field_names table maps ctor → field names in declaration order
- build_type_preamble builds a declare-datatypes preamble for record sorts,
  deduplicating against measure_preamble_sorts to avoid Z3 duplicate-sort errors
- smt_of gains optional ?resolve_field for EField(EVar x, fname) access
- return_refine_ext lifts return_refine to also carry the record sort name
- check_post extended with ?record_sort: reflects ERecord literals via
  reflect_record_literal; builds field resolver via make_field_resolver
- Spike criterion verified: {v : State | v.count >= 0} discharges on
  {count: 1}, rejects {count: -1}, conservatively skips {count: x}

Adds specs/plans/2026-06-25-refine-check-record-spike.md (implementation plan).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(stdlib): add RRB.Vec and Parallel modules

Two new stdlib modules for bulk parallel work:

- `stdlib/rrb_vec.march` (mod RRB): persistent sequence backed by List
  in v1. ptype Vec(a) / ptype Slice(a). Full API: empty, singleton,
  from_list, from_array, tabulate, range, length, is_empty, get, push,
  concat, slice, fold, chunk, to_list, to_array, each, map, fold_left.
  chunk(v, n) returns Array(Slice(a)) with no empty slices; fold over
  a Slice is O(slice length) with zero allocation.

- `stdlib/parallel.march` (mod Parallel): map-reduce over RRB.Vec via
  task_spawn/task_await_unwrap. pmap/pmap_n (order-preserving), preduce/
  preduce_n (associative reduce with explicit zero+merge), and convenience
  wrappers psum, psum_float, pcount, pany, pall. Sequential in the
  interpreter, real multi-core when compiled.

Tests: test/stdlib/test_rrb_vec.march (47 assertions). Registered in
test_stdlib_march.ml; both files added to all_stdlib_decls load list.
All 49 stdlib_march Alcotest cases pass; full suite green (321/224/786).

Docs: docs/cookbook/parallel-data.md (new chapter with worked examples:
word frequency, image filter, statistics, pfilter-via-preduce, perf
notes, interpreter vs compiled table). docs/parallelism.md updated with
RRB/Parallel section and cookbook link. stdlib count updated to 103 in
CLAUDE.md, docs/stdlib.md, specs/features/standard-library.md, and
SKILL.md. doc-lint passes.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(stdlib): add mutable RingBuf with non-sendable enforcement

Adds RingBuf(a) — a fixed-capacity, mutable, O(1) circular buffer backed
by the existing internal `ring` OCaml struct in the evaluator.

Semantics:
- Index 0 = oldest (FIFO drain order); push writes newest, overwrites
  oldest when full
- Shared-reference: all aliases see mutations (actor-owned primitive)
- Non-sendable: the typechecker rejects RingBuf values in send() payloads
  with a clear error message directing users to pass the buffer as actor
  init state instead

Changes:
- lib/eval/eval.ml: move `ring` type before `value` ADT, add VRingBuf
  constructor, ring_pop_oldest helper, and 10 builtins (make, push, pop,
  get, peek_oldest, peek_newest, size, cap, clear, to_list)
- lib/typecheck/typecheck.ml: register RingBuf(a) type signatures for all
  10 builtins; add check_sendable/non_sendable_types enforcement at ESend
- stdlib/ring_buf.march: public March API module
- test/stdlib/test_ring_buf.march: 28 tests covering all operations
- test/test_stdlib_march.ml: wire ring_buf into the stdlib test suite

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(js): Chunk B — raises externs: try/catch → Ok/Err on JS target

`raises fn f(args) : Result(T, E) = "jsExport"` from a JS module now
correctly catches JS exceptions and returns `Err` instead of propagating
the exception through the March stack.

Mechanism (js_emit.ml):
- `err_payload_ty`: extracts E from Result(T,E) in ed_ret
- `warn_err_type`: compile-time warning when E is not String (best-effort
  coercion via String(e?.message ?? e) with a note that full structured
  marshalling is deferred)
- `emit_extern_call JsModuleRaises`: emits IIFE with try/catch →
  `{ $: "Ok", _0: jsExport(args) }` / `{ $: "Err", _0: __js_err_to(e) }`
- `emit_extern_clo_str JsModuleRaises`: matching $clo wrapper with
  try/catch body
- `emit_module`: emits `const __js_err_to = (e) => String(e?.message ?? e);`
  in the preamble only when at least one raises extern is used

Tested end-to-end under node: Ok branch returns value, Err branch
captures the JS error message as a String.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(js): Chunk C — diagnostic for C-backed externs on --target js

Used C-backed externs (extern "rt", extern "c", etc.) now produce a
clear compile error on --target js instead of emitting a dangling
`const alias = c_symbol` that causes a ReferenceError at load time.

Mechanism:
- `exception Js_emit_error of string list` in js_emit.ml
- After computing `used_ed` in `emit_module`, filter for CBacked externs
  via `classify_js_extern` and raise `Js_emit_error` with a per-extern
  diagnostic message
- `bin/main.ml` wraps `emit_module` in a try/with that catches
  `Js_emit_error`, prints each message to stderr, and exits non-zero

Unused C-backed externs (already filtered from used_ed) are silently
ignored — same policy as unused JS-module externs.

Tested:
- Used CBacked extern → error message + exit 1
- Unused CBacked extern → compiles cleanly

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(js): Chunk 0 — add js_extern_kind + emit_extern_call/clo seam

Factor the two sites that Chunks A/B both need to touch through a single
routed dispatch:

- `js_extern_kind` type: JsModuleSync | JsModuleBlocking | JsModuleRaises | CBacked
- `classify_js_extern`: routes an extern_decl to its kind
- `emit_extern_call`: seam for direct call-site emission; all kinds emit a
  plain synchronous call in Chunk 0 — Chunks A/B add their arms here
- `emit_extern_clo_str`: seam for $clo wrapper string; same placeholder arms
- Move `is_js_module` earlier (before the let-rec block) so classify_js_extern
  can reference it
- Update `emit_val_impl` general-call arm to dispatch through emit_extern_call
- Update `emit_extern_bridges` $clo loop to use emit_extern_clo_str

No observable behaviour change: `js_extern_import` and `js_dom_available`
outputs are byte-identical before and after.

Also add specs/plans/2026-06-22-js-ffi-full.md (full JS FFI spec).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(codegen): L4 compiler enroll/stub — remote_ref_hashes builtin + CAS hash folding

Adds three builtins for the function-by-identity remote registry:

- remote_ref_hashes(module, fn) : (String, String) — constant-folded at
  codegen time; the emit_expr handler looks up the CAS-derived Blake3
  sig_hash/impl_hash (64-char hex) and emits a heap-allocated tuple.
  Eval path returns FNV-1a 32-char hex (deterministic, non-empty).

- remote_register_stub(impl, sig, stub) : Int — maps to march_remote_register.

- remote_count() : Int — maps to march_remote_count.

march_remote_init() is auto-emitted in @main before user code runs.

Two fixes required:
1. defun.ml: added all three to builtin_names so defun keeps them as
   EApp rather than wrapping in ECallPtr (constant-folding arm fires
   only on EApp).
2. llvm_emit.ml: top-level user-module functions have no module prefix
   in TIR fn_name ("fib" not "TestRRH.fib"); lookup tries both the
   qualified key (mod.fn) and the bare fn_name.

Tests:
- 8 eval tests in test_stdlib_march (test_remote_ref_hashes.march)
- 1 compiled native test (test/native/remote_ref_hashes.march) using
  recursive fib to survive DCE; verifies 64-char Blake3 hashes

Suites: compiler 300, eval 224, codegen 320, stdlib 766 (-q), all green.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(js): fix import ordering and add source maps

ES import statements for extern modules (e.g. `import { dirname } from
"node:path"`) previously appeared after const builtin declarations.
Split emit_extern_bridges to return (imports_str, consts_str) and
reorder assembly in emit_module so all ES imports precede any const.

Add source map v3 generation: collect function → source line from the
user AST before TIR lowering (avoiding modifying the many fn_def
construction sites), thread through emit_module as fn_lines, record
(output_line, src_line) segments during emit_fn_decl_impl, then encode
via VLQ base64 into a source map v3 JSON file. The map is written
alongside the .mjs output and referenced via sourceMappingURL.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(js): implement extern blocks for JS FFI via ES module imports

Adds JS extern support to the --target js backend. March modules can
now call JavaScript functions from any ES module using the existing
extern block syntax:

  extern "node:path" : Cap(IO.Foreign) do
    fn dirname(path: String) : String
    fn js_basename(path: String) : String = "basename"
  end

js_emit.ml changes:
- Detect JS module specifiers (npm:, https://, ./,  node:, jsr:, ...)
  vs C library names ("openssl", "dom", etc.)
- For JS modules: emit grouped `import { sym, sym2 as march_name }`
  per lib specifier; sym aliased when march name ≠ JS export name
- For runtime-backed externs (march_-prefixed C symbols): add to
  runtime_uses → imported from march_runtime.mjs; emit const alias
  when march name differs from C symbol (e.g. dom.march functions)
- Emit $clo wrappers for all used externs so they work first-class
- Lazy: bridges only emitted for externs actually called/referenced
  in the emitted code (prevents unused dom.march symbols from polluting
  non-browser JS builds that auto-load dom.march)
- Track extern usage in EApp general case and emit_atom (for first-class)

tir.ml / lower.ml: add ed_lib_name (module specifier) and ed_js_sym
(JS export name: explicit @sym or march name) to extern_decl; filled
in lower.ml at both DExtern construction sites.

Test: js_extern_import — calls node:path's dirname/basename/extname
from March via extern blocks; verifies both implicit (same name) and
explicit (= "sym") aliasing.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(refine): M-a — quantified measure recursion-equation axioms

Axiomatize @[measure] functions so the solver reasons about measure VALUES
structurally, not just symbolically. For a structurally-recursive, total measure
over a user ADT, refinecheck emits an SMT preamble: a declare-datatypes model of
the ADT (element-opaque `Elem` sort for type params), an uninterpreted measure
function, a triggered non-negativity axiom (for non-negative measures), and a
triggered recursion-equation axiom per constructor arm. A measure application in
a predicate then reflects to the datatype/function-app encoding (constructor
arguments become (ctor …) terms), so the recursion axioms fire via E-matching.

Soundness gate: a measure is axiomatized ONLY if its body is `match param do
Ctor(vars) -> … end`, the arms cover EVERY constructor (totality), and every
recursive call is on a bound pattern variable (structural recursion) and the arm
translates — else it stays symbolic (sound fallback). Solver gets a 3s per-query
timeout so a hard quantified query returns `unknown` -> definite-failure skip
(never a hang, never unsound).

Verified end-to-end (tests + --check): size(Node(Leaf,x,Leaf)) computes to 1 and
size(Node(Node(Leaf,x,Leaf),y,Leaf)) to 2, so out-of-bounds tree indices are
caught while in-bounds pass. 3 axiom tests (34 total in test_refinecheck); A0
bridge, existing suites, and stdlib regression all green.

Scope (M-a): direct-match single-arg measures over user ADTs; element values
opaque. Remaining (per spec): general body shapes, measures over built-in List,
relational postconditions, performance measurement / flag-gating.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
spec: quantified recursion-equation axioms for measures (P1b follow-up)

Careful design for axiomatizing @[measure] recursion equations into SMT so the
solver reasons about measures structurally (size(Node(l,x,r))=1+size(l)+size(r))
rather than just symbolically. Covers: declare-datatypes encoding of ADTs
(element-opaque sort for polymorphism), measure as an uninterpreted function,
per-constructor forall-axioms with E-matching triggers, preamble assembly +
cache key.

Centers the non-negotiable SOUNDNESS GATE: axioms are sound only if the measure
is total + terminating + pure, else the axiom set is inconsistent and the solver
proves anything. @[measure] must therefore be a hard error if not structurally
recursive / exhaustive / pure (elevating typecheck's existing structural-recursion
info). Recommends a Lean check of the gate. Honest about quantifier
incompleteness (definite-failure keeps it sound but unpredictable), performance,
and scope (lists -> general ADTs -> measures-calling-measures -> relational
postconditions), flag-gated until measured.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(refine): measure non-negativity inference (P1b) + richer diagnostics (P1d)

P1b follow-up — non-negativity inference: a @[measure] whose body is
syntactically non-negative (literals >= 0, sums/products of non-negatives, calls
to itself/len/already-non-negative measures across if/match/block return paths)
gets the sound `m(x) >= 0` axiom, like len. So a count measure can be used
unguarded — e.g. a `size`-returning function now verifies a `_ >= 0`
postcondition, and a `_ < 0` postcondition over `size` is caught as a definite
violation (single-pass classification in check_module; user measures still get no
axiom unless provably non-negative).

P1d — diagnostics: counterexamples are now rendered for humans (`m$x` shown as
`m(x) = v`) on BOTH precondition and postcondition violations (postcondition was
missing the model), and each violation carries a `note:` line guiding the fix
(guard the call / every return path must satisfy it).

2 new tests (31 total). len behavior, stdlib regression, and violation detection
all green. Remaining P1b: quantified recursion-equation axioms (SMT datatype
modeling — the one large follow-up), concrete unfolding of user measures over
literal constructors.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(refine): P1b — user-defined @[measure] functions

A function annotated `@[measure]` is registered as a measure, so its
applications can appear in refinement predicates alongside the builtin `len`.
Generalizes the hardcoded `len` handling: any measure m(x) reflects to a
consistent SMT symbol `m$x`, so a guard about `size(t)` discharges a precondition
about `size(t)` via path sensitivity. `len(list-literal)` keeps its concrete
evaluation; `len` keeps its non-negativity axiom while user measures get no axiom
in v1 (sound — guarded uses still verify; non-negativity inference is a follow-up).

`@[measure]` is accepted end-to-end (parse + typecheck, it's an ordinary attr).
3 tests (29 total in test_refinecheck): guarded bounds over a user `size` measure
verify, a contradicting guard is rejected, an unguarded use is skipped. len
behavior unchanged; stdlib regression + violation detection green.

v1 scope: measures reflect symbolically (no quantified recursion-equation axioms,
no concrete unfolding of user measures over literal constructors) — enough for
guarded bounds over user-defined structure (size/depth/etc.).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
spec: refinement subtyping (Path 2) — SPECULATIVE; record Path 1 progress

Path 2 spec, marked speculative throughout (not approved/scheduled): the only
known route past the higher-order ceiling is making refinements participate in
unification as a subtyping relation (emit `assumptions ∧ p ⟹ q` instead of
repr-stripping). Documents the hard parts the paper design skipped — no path
context in typecheck (S0), which unify sites are checking boundaries (S2.2), the
flipped soundness contract (definite-failure → must-prove), generalization/
linearity/error-recovery interactions, and performance — plus decision criteria
and a mandatory Lean-metatheory-first gate. Quarter-scale project, abandonment
risk; recommendation remains: ship the separate pass, do Path 1, defer this.

todos: record Path 1 progress (P1a collision-safe resolution, P1c assert-as-
assume; P1b measures + P1d diagnostics remaining) and the TRefine carry.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
spec: refinement types — state of implementation + forward design

Takes stock of the built reality (A0 SMT bridge, A1/A2 + path sensitivity +
postconditions in lib/refinecheck, TRefine carried transparently in the type)
and records the key finding from the type-integration exploration:

  transparent-repr TRefine cannot do higher-order checking — unification strips
  the refinement, so it only survives on a function's own params at direct call
  sites (exactly what the separate pass already does, with path sensitivity).
  True higher-order needs refinement SUBTYPING in unification — a separate,
  larger project.

Documents the definite-failure soundness model (report only when a predicate can
never hold) that keeps the checker false-positive-free at stdlib scale, and lays
out the fork: Path 1 (deepen the separate pass — robust call resolution, user
measures, assume/lemmas) vs Path 2 (refinement subtyping, with a Lean soundness
check first). Recommends shipping the separate pass, pursuing Path 1, and NOT
building the discarded transparent-repr infer_app consumer (2b).

Supersedes the forward-looking portions of 2026-06-20-dependent-types-refinements-design.md.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(typecheck): TRefine in the internal type (increment 1, plumbing)

Add `TRefine of ty * string * Ast.expr` to the internal type. It is transparent
to unification: `repr` strips it to the base, so base-type inference, occurs,
generalize, instantiate, exhaustiveness, and TIR lowering are unchanged.
surface_ty now BUILDS TRefine for `{T | p}` annotations (previously erased).

Blast radius was only 8 match sites (not the feared 35) precisely because almost
everything canonicalises through repr first. The predicate is carried but not yet
consumed (generalize/instantiate strip it via repr), so behavior is identical —
full suite green. Increment 2 will make generalize/instantiate preserve it and
infer_app emit obligations from the type (enabling higher-order/indirect calls).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message: docs(refine): record path sensitivity, postconditions, stdlib hardening

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(refine): path sensitivity — if/when/match guards refine preconditions

refine_check now threads a path context (conditions known true) through the
walk: `if c` adds c to the then-branch and ¬c to the else; match/clause `when`
guards add the guard. At a call site those conditions become SMT assumptions,
and variable arguments are reflected as stable constants so a guard about `i`
connects to a precondition about `i`.

Now guarded code verifies:
- `if i >= 0 do take_n(i)` and the else-branch `if i < 0 do _ else take_n(i)`
- `if i >= 0 && i < len(xs) do at(xs, i)` (full bounds)
A partial guard (no upper bound) stays conservatively silent; a guard that
contradicts the precondition is rejected. The definite-failure discharge keeps
all of this free of false positives. 5 new tests (16 total in test_refinecheck).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(refine): A2 minimal — len measure + bounds checking

Extend lib/refinecheck:
- `len` measure: len(list-literal) computed statically; len(var) is a symbolic
  non-negative const (measure axiom len >= 0).
- Cross-argument predicates: a refined param's predicate may reference sibling
  params (e.g. `_ < len(xs)`); resolved to the actual arguments at the call.
- Sound discharge: report a violation ONLY when the precondition can never hold
  (definite-failure check discharge(not goal) Verified), so unknown/symbolic
  lengths are skipped — no false positives.

Pressure tests (6, in test/test_refinecheck.ml): at([10,20,30], i) rejects 5/3/-1
(out-of-bounds, off-by-one, negative), passes 1/0, and skips an unknown-length arg.
A1b behavior unchanged (5 tests still green).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
spec: harden refinement-types design after review

Review pass fixing soundness and integration gaps in the Phase A spec:
- Soundness: drop Float-value predicates from v1 (IEEE-754 modeled as SMT
  Real is unsound at NaN/inf/rounding); restrict to Int/Bool-valued terms.
  Matrix(m,n,Float) dimensions still fully supported (Nat indices).
- Add "Refinements in the Type System": unify ignores predicates, directional
  discharge in check_expr (March has no subtyping relation), scheme carries
  predicate in ty, immutability => monotonic path context.
- Add "Sorts and Reflectable Variables": which terms can enter the logic.
- Add "Call-site instantiation": substitute actuals for formals, fresh consts
  for compound actuals, cross-argument dependent preconditions.
- Soften "closed fragment => never unknown": measure axioms are quantified.
- Single long-lived `z3 -in` process w/ push/pop instead of process-per-VC.
- Vector/Matrix: additive over TNat, not a rip-out (back-compat).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(runtime,tir): macOS green-stack SIGBUS + unsound post-Perceus empty-concat fold

Two independent native-backend bugs that together crashed any program
whose green-thread stack grew past 4 KiB and used "" ++ x copies.

1. runtime/march_scheduler.c — lazy green-thread stack growth relies on a
   PROT_NONE guard-page fault handler. On macOS arm64 a protection fault is
   KERN_PROTECTION_FAILURE -> SIGBUS, not SIGSEGV, so the SIGSEGV-only handler
   never fired and the thread died with SIGBUS (exit 138) as soon as its stack
   grew. Register the handler for SIGBUS too, accept SIGBUS regardless of
   si_code (the fault-address range check is the real safety gate), and
   re-raise the actual signal on the fatal path.

2. lib/tir/simplify.ml — the identity folds x ++ "" -> x and "" ++ x -> x run
   in the Opt coordinator, which executes AFTER Perceus. Perceus accounts for
   the concat as a fresh owned allocation distinct from x and emits a dec_rc
   for both; folding them to a single alias leaves two decrements on one
   object -> RC underflow / double-free. Removed the folds (eliminating an
   RC-tracked allocation post-Perceus is unsound).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(opt): P10 Phase 3 — NativeArray-backed DataFrame Int/Float columns

Migrate TypedArray(Int)/TypedArray(Float) column storage to NativeIntArr/NativeFloatArr
in stdlib/dataframe.march. Unboxed OCaml native arrays eliminate per-element boxing
overhead on numeric column operations; LLVM path can auto-vectorize sum/map loops.

New builtins:
- native_int_arr_filter_mask(arr, mask) → filter NativeIntArr by TypedArray(Bool)
- native_float_arr_filter_mask(arr, mask) → filter NativeFloatArr by TypedArray(Bool)

Updated 20+ sites in dataframe.march: col_len, col_value_at, col_to_value_list,
filter_col_by_indices, filter_col_by_mask, builder_to_column, get_int_col,
get_float_col, float_list, eval_col_expr (LitInt/LitFloat, arithmetic),
col_from_idx_opts, col_describe_column, col_z_score, col_normalize,
col_add_float, col_mul_float, col_add_col, fill_null variants, window ops.

StrCol/BoolCol and null bitmaps stay as TypedArray (no native string/bool array).
All 780 tests pass.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
refactor(typecheck): rename opts no_panic → cap no_panic

Replace the `opts` keyword with a compound lexer token `CAP_NO_PANIC`
matching `"cap" whitespace+ "no_panic"`. This keeps `cap` usable as a
plain identifier (e.g. capability parameters) while reserving the
two-word phrase as the module directive.

Changes:
- lexer: remove OPTS from keyword table; add CAP_NO_PANIC compound rule
- parser: rename production opts_decl → cap_no_panic_decl; emit DOpts(["no_panic"])
- ast: update DOpts comment
- typecheck: update all error messages and comments to cap no_panic
- stdlib: revert regex.march (ropts→opts) and dataframe.march (wopts→opts)
  now that opts is no longer a reserved keyword

  check CAP_NO_PANIC token; update all March source strings
- specs: update todos, progress, capability-system-phase3, tier1_impl_plan,
  optimization_todos to reflect the rename

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(opt): P13 EField-of-known-record folding + P14 boolean simplify peepholes

P13 — lib/tir/cprop.ml
  Add a separate `field_env` that tracks ERecord / EUpdate / alias
  bindings alongside the existing literal env.  EField(AVar r, k) folds
  to the stored atom when r is in fenv; EUpdate merges new fields over
  the base record's known fields so `{r with y=99}.x` still folds `x`
  from the base.  RC operations are never substituted; AVar/ADefRef
  aliases are NOT propagated in the main literal env (avoids ECallPtr
  dispatch bugs in LLVM emit).  2 new tests in the `cprop` group.

P14 — lib/tir/simplify.ml
  if x then true else false  → x
  if x then false else true  → not x
  x == x → true  (non-float; IEEE 754 NaN excluded)
  x != x → false (non-float)
  5 new tests in the `simplify` group.

specs/todos.md: mark P13 + P14 done; P11 + P12 remain.

All 780 stdlib tests pass; 274 codegen tests pass.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(typecheck): Phase 3a — explicit bounded type parameters

Adds `fn f[s : Bound](param : Type(s)) : ...` syntax for constraining
type variables to constructors of a named ADT, known interfaces, or Nat.

Parser: `fn_bound_param` nonterminal — optional `[name : Ty, ...]` bracket
clause between function name and `(`, for both `fn` and `pfn`.

AST: `fn_bounds : (string * ty) list` field on `DFn`/`DPFn`.

Typecheck: `check_fn` registers each bound var in `fn_tvars` (so it
resolves as a type variable in param/return annotations) and queues
constraints — `CADTBound` for user ADTs, `CInterface` for known
interfaces (Eq/Ord/Show/…), `CTNatBound` for `Nat` — into
`pending_constraints`. `discharge_constraints` checks concrete-typed
vars at the declaration boundary; polymorphic tvars are skipped.
Error: "`Int` is not a variant of `ConnState`."

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(typecheck): Phase 2c/2d — Tagged(X,T) specialization tags + env records

Add `Tagged(X, T)` as a builtin two-arg type constructor for phantom
specialization policies (realtime, SIMD width, platform targeting).

Compiler changes (lib/typecheck/typecheck.ml):
- `("Tagged", 2)` added to builtin_types — `Tagged(DSP, Realtime)` etc.
  are now valid type annotations everywhere
- `("Alloc", 0)` and `("Panic", 0)` added as future capability roots
- `cap_paths_in_surface_ty` skips `Tagged` args — prevents the capability
  needs-checker from treating the inner tag types as capability requirements
- Check 7 in `check_module_needs`: a function with `Tagged(_, Realtime)` in
  its parameter list cannot also take `Cap(Alloc)`, `Cap(IO)`, or `Cap(Panic)`.
  This is the realtime exclusion narrowing rule from §3/§5 of the capability
  spec — realtime functions are statically proven not to allocate, do IO, or abort.

Tests (test/test_compiler.ml):
- `Tagged(X,T): valid type annotation` — parses and typechecks cleanly
- `Tagged(_, Realtime) + Cap(IO): error` — Check 7 fires
- `Tagged(_, Realtime) + Cap(Alloc): error` — Check 7 fires
- `Tagged(_, Realtime) + Cap(Panic): error` — Check 7 fires
- `Tagged(_, Standard): no exclusion` — non-realtime tags are unrestricted

Phase 2d (environment records) has no compiler work — records of caps are
already expressible. Documented in docs/capabilities.md as the pattern for
bundling caps into records, narrowing via record construction, and testing
with function fields alongside cap fields.

214 compiler tests pass, 2 pre-existing parser-gaps failures unchanged.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
chore(test): reliable parallel-agent test execution

Four improvements:

1. test/dune: add `-e` (show-errors) to all 4 runner stanzas so failure
   details appear inline rather than requiring log-file archaeology.

2. scripts/run-tests.sh: new wrapper that separates build (dune RPC) from
   execution (direct binary). Agents run this to avoid RPC contention:
   `dune build` serialises safely; `_build/.../run_*.exe -e` runs without
   any dune daemon involvement.  Optional MARCH_DUNE_SHUTDOWN=1 calls
   `dune shutdown` first to clear a stale daemon.  Per-suite timeout via
   `timeout(1)` / `gtimeout` when available.

3. CLAUDE.md: document --no-buffer, --force, direct binary invocation, and
   `dune shutdown` under a "Parallel-agent / reliable test invocation" heading.

Item 5 (stdlib TCenv warmup): measured — the race does not exist. Test
runners call check_module/base_env directly; the repl.ml tcenv cache is
REPL-only and never written during dune runtest. No implementation needed.

  test_eval.ml          — eval/runtime suites
  test_codegen.ml       — TIR/JIT/LLVM suites

Each module becomes its own dune library (march_test_compiler, etc.),
so editing one suite no longer triggers recompilation of the others.
All 4 runners (202 + 214 + 267 + 780 = 1463 tests) pass.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(forge): dep scoping — [dev-only-deps] and [test-deps]

Introduce two new dependency sections alongside the existing [dev-deps]:

  [deps]          ships to prod; available in all environments
  [dev-deps]      dev + test (shared non-prod tooling) — NOW WIRED
  [dev-only-deps] dev only (hot reload, dashboards, dev proxies)
  [test-deps]     test only (test frameworks, fuzz libs, mocking)

Previously, [dev-deps] was parsed but never placed on MARCH_LIB_PATH
for either forge build or forge test. This commit wires all four
buckets correctly:

  forge build (dev)     → deps + dev-deps + dev-only-deps
  forge build --release → deps only
  forge test            → deps + dev-deps + test-deps

Also:
- Extracts dep_to_lib_paths helper in cmd_build.ml (eliminates
  duplication between cmd_build and cmd_test path expansion)
- forge deps now fetches all four buckets
- forge add --dev-only / --test write to the new sections
- 2 new tests in test_resolver.ml (inline + section form parsing)
- Spec: specs/plans/2026-06-16-compilation-environments.md

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(cli): march check reports type errors from auto-discovered imported modules

run_check_cmd filtered diagnostics against the command-line file list
only, so errors in modules pulled in by import resolution (source dir /
MARCH_LIB_PATH siblings) were dropped as stdlib-internal — the same
class of bug as the a8ca488 imported-module filter regression, on a
third code path (march check <files>, used by forge).

Each entry's user_files (which carry parse-time span paths since the
resolver fix) is now merged into the membership filter.  Verified:
march check imports/entry_imports_ill_typed.march exits 1 rendering the
imported module's arity error; well-typed entries still exit 0.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message: Merge branch 'main' into claude/eager-williams-af8b7d

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(cache): content-hash-keyed artifacts + atomic writes for shared ~/.cache/march

The cache dir is shared across worktrees and concurrent sessions; two
fixed-name artifacts let sessions corrupt each other:

- libmarch_runtime.so (ensure_runtime_so) was invalidated only by
  march_runtime.c's mtime — edits to the other runtime C files never
  invalidated it, and a worktree with diverged sources overwrote it with
  an ABI-mismatched binary, hanging whichever session dlopen'd it.
- libmarch_rt_test.so (setup_jit_runtime) had an existence-only check
  and was NEVER invalidated (observed: 22-minute test_march.exe hangs).

Both are now keyed by a digest of every compiled .c file, every
runtime/*.h, and (for the runtime) the clang flag string — identical
inputs share one artifact, divergent worktrees get separate files.

All five cache writers (those two plus stdlib_prelude .so/.names,
stdlib_ast .bin, stdlib_tcenv .bin) now compile/marshal to a
pid-suffixed temp and rename(2) into place, so readers never see a
half-written file. The prelude .names publishes before its .so, so a
visible .so always implies a complete names file. ensure_runtime_so
with no source tree falls back to the newest cached runtime .so.

Verified: fresh/warm REPL runs on an isolated HOME, a runtime source
edit produces a new key leaving the old artifact intact, JIT alcotest
group green, full suite green (1431 tests).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message: Merge branch 'main': take 78afbbc resolver fix; keep libpath auto-discovery regression + emit_case guard

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(resolver): include as-parsed paths in user_files so relative MARCH_LIB_PATH diagnostics are not dropped

Whole-program compilation accepted ill-typed code the single-file checker
rejects: user_files held only realpath-canonicalised paths while diagnostic
spans carry the as-parsed (often relative) path, so is_user_file's exact
string match failed for every lib-path module and their type errors were
tolerated as stdlib-internal. Type-incorrect TIR then reached codegen and
died in clang with undefined pattern-binder symbols (i64 scrutinee on
emit_case's integer-switch path never binds br_vars).

- resolver: record the as-parsed path spelling alongside the canonical one;
  user_files is the union of both.
- llvm_emit (defense-in-depth): emit_case fails loudly when an ADT ctor
  branch with binders meets a non-pointer scrutinee instead of emitting an
  integer switch with unbound binder symbols.
- new regression: test/libpath_ill_typed/ — relative MARCH_LIB_PATH module
  with a body type error, never imported by the entry, must fail --check.
  (The existing entry_imports_ill_typed rules caught this only under
  --force; dune's incremental cache replayed their stale pass.)

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(cas): include source-dir imports in the early --check/--compile hash

The early source-hash CAS key covered the entry file's source, the stdlib
hash, and all .march files under MARCH_LIB_PATH — but not sibling modules
resolved from the entry file's own directory, even though the resolver's
search path is source_dir :: lib_paths.  Editing an imported sibling after
a clean run left the hash unchanged, so the next --check hit the stale
"check ok" artifact and exited 0 on a now-ill-typed program (and --compile
reused a stale binary).

Hash every .march file under the entry's directory the same way lib-path
dirs are hashed.  Extra files in the key can only cause spurious misses,
never stale hits.

Regression rule: test/imports/cas_stale/ checks a clean copy, swaps the
imported module for an ill-typed version, and asserts the second --check
exits nonzero (verified to fail against the pre-fix compiler).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(resolver): report type errors from relatively-pathed lib modules

user_files held only realpath-canonicalised paths (since the LSP/CLI
resolution unification) while files are parsed under their as-given
path -- diagnostic spans carry the as-given string, so with a relative
MARCH_LIB_PATH (or relative source-dir imports) is_user_file's exact
List.mem failed for every lib-module diagnostic and the errors were
tolerated as stdlib-internal. Ill-typed whole-program builds proceeded
into codegen: the depot repro had 70 swallowed type errors and failed
only at clang with "use of undefined value '@$f27180'" (an Ok(v)
pattern binder never bound because the ill-typed Bool scrutinee took
emit_case's integer-switch path, which binds no br_vars). The
pre-existing entry_imports_ill_typed fixtures had silently become live
failures for the same reason.

user_files now records BOTH spellings of every loaded file
(record_user_path at the entry pre-mark, the explicit-import load path,
and auto-discovery phase 2, which threads the as-given path through
from phase 1); loaded_paths stays canonical-only for dedup.

Regression: test/imports/lib_path_ill_typed/ -- relative MARCH_LIB_PATH,
ill-typed lib module never called by the entry; --check and march test
must both exit 1 (verified exit 0 pre-fix). Full suite green.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(cas): include source-dir sibling modules in the early-exit cache hash

The early source-hash CAS in `compile` hashed the entry file, the stdlib
hash, and all .march files under MARCH_LIB_PATH — but not the sibling
.march files in the entry's own source directory, which resolve_imports
auto-discovers and loads as user code (search_path = source_dir :: lib
paths). Editing an imported sibling to introduce a type error therefore
hit the stale OK artifact: --check/--compile exited 0 without ever
typechecking the now-ill-typed program. The cache-level twin of the
diagnostic-filter regression fixed in 78afbbc.

The hash walk now covers Filename.dirname entry :: MARCH_LIB_PATH dirs
(same sorted collect_lib_files walk), skipping the realpath-equal entry
file whose bytes are already the first buffer element. Adding or
deleting a sibling also changes the key (paths are hashed alongside
contents); reverting an edit re-hits the original OK artifact.

Regression rule in test/dune (cas_stale_dir): generates a two-file
sandbox, --check (caches OK), injects an arity error into the sibling,
and requires the second --check to exit 1 — a warm-cache exit 0 fails
the rule. Files are generated inline so the duplicate `mod CsHelper`
bodies never pollute imports/ auto-discovery for other rules.

Verified: fresh-dir repro flips 0→1 after the sibling edit and back to a
cache hit on revert; `dune build @runtest --force` exits 0.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(resolver): report user_files under the paths diagnostics actually carry

Regression from 2545039 (resolver unification): the shared resolver dedups
loaded files by canonical (realpath) path and returned those canonical
strings as user_files, but files are parsed under the possibly-relative
path they were opened with — which is what diagnostic spans carry.  The
CLI's is_user_file membership check (List.mem span.file user_files) then
missed every imported-module diagnostic, silently restoring the pre-a8ca488
bug: type errors in imported user modules were filtered as stdlib-internal
and march --check / march test exited 0 on ill-typed programs.

Track the parse-path strings (plus their canonical forms, for callers whose
spans are absolute) in a separate table and return those as user_files;
dedup stays keyed on canonical paths.  Auto-discovery now threads the
original parse path through phase 1 instead of dropping it.

Caught by the existing a8ca488 regression rule: dune runtest asserts
march --check imports/entry_imports_ill_typed.march exits 1.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(resolver): report imported-module type errors again — user_files must carry parse-time paths

Regression of a8ca488 ("report type errors from imported user modules"),
introduced one day later by 2545039 (LSP/CLI resolver unification): when
resolve_imports was consolidated into lib/resolver, the explicit-import
dedup was canonicalised with Unix.realpath, so the returned user_files
list held canonical ABSOLUTE paths while diagnostic spans carry the
RELATIVE path each file was parsed under (pos_fname). bin/main.ml's
is_user_file does an exact List.mem of span files against user_files, so
imported-module errors never matched, were filtered as stdlib-internal,
and ill-typed programs compiled to corrupt binaries with exit 0 and no
diagnostics.

The regression fixture test/imports/entry_imports_ill_typed.march caught
this immediately — its two with-accepted-exit-codes-1 dune rules failed
from 2545039 onward — but the failures were misread as pre-existing noise
because the test-suite tail looked green; only dune runtest's exit code
showed it.

Fix: track user_files separately from the canonical-keyed loaded_paths
dedup table, recording BOTH the parse-time path string (what spans carry)
and the realpath at all three load sites: the entry pre-mark, explicit
`load`, and auto-discovery (phase 1 now threads the original path through
to phase 2 alongside canon_fp). Dedup behaviour is unchanged.

Verified: --check and `march test` on the fixture exit 1 (cold and warm
CAS cache) with the error rendered against the imported file's source;
`dune build @runtest --force` exits 0 with every rule re-executed.

Known adjacent gap (deliberately not fixed here, tracked separately): the
early-CAS --check/--compile source hash omits source-dir sibling files,
so editing an imported sibling can serve a stale cached OK.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(codegen): restore erased-closure pointers in ECallPtr dispatch

A closure parameter captured into another lambda's environment loses its
TFn type through the erased env slot (TVar -> i64). ECallPtr dispatch
assumed the callee atom was already ptr and fed the raw i64 SSA value to
the fn-pointer getelementptr -- clang rejected the module ('%ldNN'
defined with type 'i64' but expected 'ptr'). Hit by depot's
Depot.Migration.run pattern: __try_call(fn _ -> thunk()).

Every ptr->i64 erasure goes through coerce's ashr-by-1 (the tagged-
immediate untag), so an erased i64 callee holds p>>1. Heap pointers are
even, so shl 1 -- WITHOUT the |1 immediate tag coerce would add --
restores the original pointer exactly (coerce would yield p+1: a
misaligned non-pointer, jumping to garbage).

Fixture: test/imports/erased_clo_native/ (compile-and-run, distilled
from the depot repro). Suite green; list_ops bench output correct.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(cli,forge,typecheck): sort directory entries for deterministic file discovery

Raw Sys.readdir order is unspecified and filesystem-dependent, so
MARCH_LIB_PATH module discovery (collect_lib_files) varied across
machines — pre-fix bugs sensitive to module load order (e.g. the
unqualified-variant ordering leak fixed in 8d83950) reproduced on some
filesystems and not others. Sort entries at every .march discovery walk:

- bin/main.ml: collect_lib_files (MARCH_LIB_PATH), march_files_in (fmt),
  march test discovery
- forge: cmd_build find_march_files, cmd_test find_test_files,
  cmd_lint/cmd_format collect_march_files
- typecheck: suggest_module_name tie-breaking

forge resolver_api_surface/resolver_cas_package already sorted.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(eval): align FileKind OtherKind VCon tag with ptype declaration

file_stat emitted VCon ("Other", []) but stdlib FileKind declares the
constructor as OtherKind. Pattern matches on OtherKind would never fire
at runtime. Corrected to VCon ("OtherKind", []).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(test): migrate wire_encode_test to uncurried collection API

Map.fold and List.fold_left now take collection first, acc second,
uncurried callback last. Also add missing | in DescribeTarget variants.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(codegen,runtime): unify tuple/erased slot convention; conditional untag preserves type-erased heap values

Compiled code read tuple slots as ptr and untagged scalar views with an
unconditional ashr, but ETuple/EAlloc-fallback/EStackAlloc-fallback/EReuse
stored scalars in their NATURAL repr — match [("a",5)] do Cons((k,v),_)->v
returned 2 and true matched as false in every compiled build.  All tuple and
erased-cell slots now use the UNIFORM convention (scalars tagged via coerce
i64->ptr, heap values raw), matching the destructure fallbacks that were
already reading them that way.

coerce ptr->i64 now untags CONDITIONALLY (ashr iff low bit set): producers
always tag scalars, so an even value in a scalar-typed view is a heap pointer
flowing through a lying static type (dynamically-typed record/alist code,
e.g. depot's heterogeneous schema opts).  Preserving even bits verbatim makes
tag->untag lossless for all bit patterns, so such values survive scalar-typed
transit (the depot 'blank uses explicit defaults' hang: a string payload
halved by ashr then memcmp'd with a garbage length).

march_extras.c record builtins aligned with the uniform convention:
- record_entries pair snd: uniform (was natural — true read back as false)
- march_record_field_dyn: tags 'i' fields (consumers cond-untag)
- record_put: normalizes input, downgrades kind i->g when a scalar-typed
  value's bits are heap-plausible (lying static type) so bits round-trip
- record_from_list: pair values arrive uniform; per-field kind normalization

Depot test_depot_schema natively: was 3 F + hang at test 17, now 42 tests,
6 failures (all generic-to_string printing, separate bugs).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(codegen,runtime): native lowering for record introspection builtins via runtime shape registry

The seven record introspection builtins (record_keys, record_values,
record_entries, record_get, record_put, record_has_key, record_from_list)
were interpreter-only: native builds emitted bare 'call ptr
@record_entries(...)' with no definition, failing at link time.

Records carry no field names at runtime, so this adds a record shape
registry to runtime/march_extras.c: every record allocation (ERecord /
EUpdate / EReuse) stamps an interned shape id into the previously-unused
header pad word (offset 12, always zero via march_alloc's calloc).  A shape
descriptor is the canonical string "name:kind;..." (fields sorted by name,
kind chars i/f/p/g = raw-i64 scalar / double bits / heap ptr / generic).
The builtins are C functions reading the descriptor; record_put with a new
key and record_from_list intern NEW shapes at runtime (sorted insert), so
dynamically-built records (depot's Depot.Schema.blank fold over
record_from_list([])) work in compiled code.

Value conventions (documented in march_extras.c):
- record slots hold the natural representation; the descriptor records kind
- outputs into generic ADT slots (values list payloads, record_get's Some
  payload) low-bit-tag ints, matching the ashr-untag on extraction
- record_entries pair snd stays natural per field kind (tuple slots are
  read at concrete element types without untagging)
- record_put / record_from_list call sites pass explicit kind hints

llvm_emit.ml: EApp special cases for the seven builtins (natural-repr value
passing + kind args), ECallPtr redirect, builtin_ret_ty/mangle_extern
entries, preamble declares, shape stamping at ERecord/EUpdate/EReuse,
stack-alloc pad zeroing, and EField on statically-unknown record types now
emits a dynamic by-name lookup (march_record_field_dyn; legacy slot-0 read
for shape-0 cells).  WASM targets skip shape metadata.  borrow.ml: record_*
args are borrowed.  eval.ml: record_keys/values/entries now sort by field
name to match the native sorted-by-name slot layout.

Two enabling fixes shipped together (both blocked depot's native build):
- lower.ml rename_tir_vars is now scope-aware: a local binder sharing a
  module fn name (e.g. a param 'fields' in a module defining fn fields/1)
  no longer has its references rewritten to the qualified global, which
  compiled to an undefined zero-arg call '@Depot.Schema.fields()'.
- ensure_adt_eq_fn generates element-wise short-circuiting structural ==
  for TTuple and TRecord (previously pointer comparison, so
  List.member(record_entries(r), ("age", 30)) was always false natively).

Fixture: test/imports/record_native/ (18 compile-and-run checks: static
shapes, heterogeneous entries via List.member, runtime shape extension,
dynamic blank-style fold + dynamic field reads).  dune runtest green
(1422 alcotest + doctests + fixtures; the 2 entry_imports_ill_typed rule
failures are pre-existing on this branch).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(tir): locals shadow sibling module-level fns in rename_tir_vars

rename_tir_vars qualified every AVar whose name matched a sibling
module-level fn, ignoring local binders. A param like `fields` in
pfn blank_fold(fields, acc) next to fn fields(schema) was rewritten
to the qualified global (Depot.Schema.fields), which llvm_emit then
emitted as the fn's address used as data (SIGBUS) or a zero-arg "gl"
call ("use of undefined value" at clang time).

The rename walk now threads an in-scope binder set — fn params,
ELet/ELetRec bindings, ECase branch vars — and skips shadowed names.
TIR-level analog of 02d212d ("local fns shadow bulk imports").

Regression fixture test/imports/param_shadow_native/ compiles natively
and runs; covers param, match-pattern, and let-binding shadowing.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message: chore: sync decision graph export after merge

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(cas): invalidate compiled-artifact cache when runtime C sources change

compilation_hash mixed in compiler identity (83ef546) but nothing about
runtime/*.c|*.h, which clang compiles and links into the output binary at
march-compile time. Editing runtime/march_extras.c still served the stale
binary cached under the same key. Mix a BLAKE3 digest of the runtime
sources (sorted name + contents, Lazy per process) into compilation_hash,
resolving the runtime dir with the same candidates main.ml uses.

Also fix the cache-hit paths in bin/main.ml ignoring the cp exit code:
a dead artifact pointer (stored pointers hold the original output path,
so deleting that file orphans them) printed "compiled (cached)" while
writing no output. New Cas.copy_artifact returns false on missing source
or failed copy — both call sites now fall through and recompile — and
treats src==dest (same inode, recompile with the same -o) as success,
since cp exits non-zero on identical files.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(typecheck): hmac_sha256 signature now matches eval/runtime/llvm_emit

The typecheck entry declared Bytes -> Bytes -> Bytes, but every
implementation disagreed: the eval builtin returns Ok(Bytes), the native
runtime (march_hmac_sha256) reads march_string args and returns
Result(Bytes, String), and llvm_emit's builtin_ret_ty already said
Result(Bytes, String). Correct usage (Ok/Err match on String args) was
rejected; the only accepted usage would misbehave at runtime.

Canonical signature: String -> String -> Result(Bytes, String) — same as
the existing stdlib_hmac_sha256 entry. hmac_sha256_bytes (Bytes -> Bytes
-> Bytes, bare result) was already consistent across all sites; unchanged.

Adds typecheck-level regression tests for both builtins — prior tests
called the eval builtin directly and never exercised the signature.
1424 -> 1426 tests.

Merge branch 'claude/peaceful-brahmagupta-981324'

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(bench,test): restore | separators in type declarations over-removed by 33bf2a2

Commit 33bf2a2 (remove | from match arms) also stripped | from type
variant declarations in 5 places. Two were already fixed; restore the
remaining three: bench/tree_transform.march, bench/parallel.march,
test/depot/wire_encode_test.march.

Verified: dune runtest (147 tests pass), tree_transform and parallel
compile at --opt 2, tree_transform runs and prints 104857600.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(runtime): tag Int payloads in C-built generic list cells (extras/compress)

Compiled code uses uniform low-bit integer tagging ((n<<1)|1) for Int
payloads in generic constructor slots and emits ashr #1 on extraction.
march_http.c's make_int_cons already pre-tags, but the int_cons /
bytes_from_raw / bytes_to_raw helpers in march_extras.c and the copies in
march_compress.c stored and read raw i64s, so every C builtin exchanging
Bytes/List(Int) with compiled code corrupted values in both directions
(observed: base64_encode(Bytes([1,2,255])) -> [3,5,255] on input,
hmac output bytes halved on read-back). Interp mode was unaffected.

Verified: base64/hmac native == interp == python; RFC 5869 HKDF vector
passes natively; full suite green.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(llvm): nested Bool/Int/Atom literal patterns miscompiled as heap tag switches

Literal patterns nested inside constructor patterns (e.g. Ok(true)/Ok(false))
compiled the inner test as a heap ctor-tag switch on the bound field. But ADT
fields holding immediates are stored low-bit-tagged ((n<<1)|1), not as heap
pointers — the tag load was a wild dereference. With the switch's default
being unreachable, LLVM folded the test away: the first bool arm was always
taken (T T E instead of T F E); nested Int/Atom literal matches segfaulted.

Literal tags are syntactically unambiguous (lowercase true/false, integers,
:atoms — ADT ctors are capitalized identifiers), so emit_case now detects
all-immediate-literal-tag cases and never takes the load-ctor-tag path:

- Bool: reuse the existing untag (ashr) + trunc + br i1 path.
- Int/Atom: switch on the raw ptrtoint bits against (lit<<1)|1 labels.
  Comparing tagged-vs-tagged is exact; untagging via ashr would lose bit 63
  of 64-bit FNV1a atom hashes.

User ADTs literally named True | False(Int) still take the ctor-tag switch.
Also fixes the latent miscompile in stdlib/check.march format_error, which
printed the right string only by accident of arm order.

Regression tests (nested_lit_pattern_codegen): IR-level assertions that
exactly one i32 ctor-tag switch (the outer Ok/Err) survives, bool fields
are tested via trunc to i1, and int literals compare against tagged
immediates. 1421 -> 1424 tests. binary_trees benchmark unchanged (~290ms).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(lsp): unify module resolution and typechecking with the CLI

Editor diagnostics diverged from `march --check` in two ways, so Zed showed
"Unknown module `Bastion`" (and ~30 more errors per file) on projects that
forge check reports clean:

1. resolver: bin/main.ml had grown its own resolve_imports while the shared
   lib/resolver copy (used by the LSP and REPL) was an old fork with no
   MARCH_LIB_PATH auto-discovery — modules whose declared name doesn't match
   their filename (mod Bastion.Channel in channel.march) were unresolvable
   in the editor.  The full implementation (auto-discovery with declared-name
   indexing, canonical-path dedup, dotted module filenames, user_files
   tracking) now lives in lib/resolver as the single source of truth;
   bin/main.ml delegates to it.  New ?auto_discover flag (default true);
   the REPL passes false to keep its per-input resolution cheap.  The
   import-path dedup is now canonicalised too, fixing the relative-entry
   double-load.

2. typecheck: the LSP calls check_module_full, which was a REDUCED duplicate
   of check_module's pass 1 — no type/ctor/record prebinding, no pass 1b —
   so qualified type annotations (Bastion.Channel.ChannelConn) failed to
   resolve only in the LSP.  check_module and check_module_full now share
   one implementation (check_module_core) that returns the final env.

Verified by driving march-lsp over JSON-RPC against bastion: didOpen on
channel_server/router/test_channel/csrf now publishes 0 errors (was 34 on
channel_server alone).  Full suite green (147 LSP tests incl.); forge check
still clean on bastion (80 files) and depot (27 files).

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(runtime): tag __try_call's Bool result so compiled Check.all properties pass

Compiled property tests failed on run 1 with "returned false" for
trivially-true properties while the interpreter passed them. __try_call
(the panic-catching trampoline behind Check.try_prop) stored the
property thunk's raw i64 Bool directly into the Result Ok field, but
compiled March reads polymorphic ADT immediate fields with the uniform
low-bit tag convention (ptrtoint + ashr 1): Ok(1) read back as 0, so
Check.is_failing saw not(false) = failing for every passing property.

Fix: store ((result << 1) | 1) at the Result construction point in
__try_call, matching the tagged-immediate convention, and tighten the
builtin's typechecker signature from (Bool -> a) -> Result(a, String)
to (Bool -> Bool) -> Result(Bool, String) so any future heap-typed use
fails to typecheck instead of being silently corrupted by the tagging.
The interpreter's __try_call builds OCaml values and is unaffected.

End-to-end regression test compiles a trivially-true Check.all property
through the real --compile --test pipeline and asserts the binary exits
0; skips gracefully when clang/runtime is unavailable (JIT-test
convention). 1422 -> 1423 tests.

Found during verification, deferred as separate work: Bool literal
patterns nested in constructor patterns (Ok(true)/Ok(false)) compile to
a heap-tag switch on a tagged immediate and LLVM's unreachable-default
folding makes the first Bool arm unconditionally taken — a real
miscompile, tracked separately.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(lower): qualify sibling-fn refs in DMod test bodies so DCE keeps closure-only helpers

Multi-test-file `forge test` builds load non-entry test files as DMod
entries. lower_mod_decls registers their functions qualified
(TestB.helper) and renames refs inside the module's own DFn bodies via
rename_tir_vars — but collect_tests lowered test/setup/setup_all bodies
with plain lower_expr, leaving refs unqualified (helper). A helper
referenced only through a closure (e.g. a Check.all property lambda)
then miscompiled down the pipeline: defun captured it as a free
variable instead of resolving a top-level fn, DCE pruned the qualified
definition (reachability matches free_vars against fn names — name
mismatch), and llvm_emit's no-slot AVar fallback emitted a
@helper$clo_wrap trampoline calling a dangling @helper:

    test/markdown_test.ll: error: use of undefined value '@all_in'

Fix: collect_tests threads the enclosing module prefix + direct fn/let
names through DMod descent and applies rename_tir_vars to lowered
test/setup bodies, mirroring the module's DFn handling.

Regression test drives a DMod test that references a sibling helper
through a closure across the full native pipeline and asserts no
dangling unqualified trampoline survives. 1421 -> 1422 tests.

Note: compiled Check.all properties still fail at runtime (returned
false, counterexample prints as #<tag:0>) while the interpreter passes
— separate pre-existing bug, tracked separately.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(typecheck): resolve cross-module named-type leaks in whole-program builds

`forge build` typechecks each lib file individually (`march --check <file>`)
with the siblings discovered via MARCH_LIB_PATH, so any file can be the compile
entry: its declarations sit at the top level while the imported modules are
wrapped `DMod`s.  That asymmetry leaked several cross-module type errors that a
single-entry `forge test` build never hits, so well-typed projects (sigil)
failed to build while their tests passed.

Four fixes, all order-preserving so constructor tags are unchanged at lowering:

- Order sibling modules by UNQUALIFIED type/constructor references, not just
  qualified `Mod.x` ones.  A module using another's variant type or constructors
  by their bare name (`List(Block)`, `Heading(..)`, `match .. Heading(x) ->`)
  now records a dependency on the definer, so the definer is checked first and
  its bare names are exported before the user is checked ("I cannot find
  `Block`").  This reorders only; it does not pre-bind names eagerly, which would
  change a constructor's resolved type and miscompile its tag.

- Disambiguate a constructor shared by several ADTs (`Text` in both `Inline` and
  `XmlNode`) by the expected/scrutinee type instead of `lookup_ctor`'s
  order-dependent "most-recently-registered wins".  `infer_pattern` now threads
  the scrutinee type from both match-checking paths and from nested constructor
  arguments.

- Pre-bind the entry module's own top-level declarations under its qualified
  module name (`Config.Site`), as a wrapped sibling already is.  Without this a
  sibling's `Config.Site` annotation falls through to the module registry, which
  carries no record fields and so yields a nominal `TCon` that will not unify
  with the entry module's structural use of the record.

- Register the disambiguated module.type.ctor form (`Md.Inline.Text`) in pass 1
  with the bare type as its ci_type, so a sibling can reference the entry
  module's constructors in their fully-qualified disambiguating form.

Regression: test/whole_program fixtures + two `march --check` rules in test/dune
(record owned by the entry, variant used unqualified by an earlier-sorted
sibling) — both fail before and pass after.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message: docs(syntax-ref): remove | as match arm separator from docs

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
docs(readme): fix stale match/if syntax throughout

match now uses `do...end` not `with`, arms no longer require leading `|`,
and conditionals use `if cond do ... end` not `if cond then ...`.

Updated 5 match expressions and 2 if expressions across the language tour
and FBIP example.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(llvm): last-wins entry point selection for multi-module compilation

Change List.find_map to List.fold_left so that when multiple modules
each define fn main(), the entry file's module wins.  The entry file's
declarations are injected last into mod_decls (and therefore appear
last in tm_fns), so fold_left (last match wins) gives the correct
result without requiring the caller to control declaration order.

Without this fix, MARCH_LIB_PATH compilation with a library that also
defines main (e.g. Ssg.main in sigil.march) would pick the library's
main instead of the user's entry file's main, causing the wrong program
to run.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(runtime): fix two RC bugs in march_task_spawn_thunk

Bug 1 (crash): task[0] = 0 zeroed the Task object's ref-count field
immediately after march_alloc set it to 1. When the caller's Perceus-emitted
march_decrc_local fired on the Task result, rc went 0→-1, triggering
"local RC underflow — aborting".

Bug 2 (leak): march_incrc(clo_ptr) added a spurious extra reference to the
closure. Perceus treats task_spawn as a consuming call and emits no DecRC on
the caller side, so the closure's rc=1 IS the task's reference. Adding another
IncRC left the closure with rc=1 after the trampoline's march_decrc ran, leaking
it for the program's lifetime.

Fix: remove task[0]=0 (march_alloc already initialises rc=1, tag=0, pad=0),
remove the redundant ((int32_t*)task)[2]=0 (same reason), and remove the
spurious march_incrc. The Task's rc field is now correctly 1 when returned
to the caller, and the closure's single reference is properly consumed by the
trampoline's march_decrc after the lambda returns.

Pure-integer tasks were unaffected because no heap-allocating RC operations
fired before the crash point in the main thread.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(perceus): exclude TPtr sources from is_borrowed_field to fix closure FV RC underflow

When borrow inference marks a closure's \$clo parameter as :borrow (not :own),
it lands in the outer borrowed set passed to insert_rc, which is unioned into
borrowed' (live_after).  The is_borrowed_field check in ELet then fires for
any field access "let fv = \$clo.fvN" because \$clo is in live_after (condition 1),
and marks the closure FV as a _borrowed_field_var.  This suppresses the EIncRC
that d2cf09e was supposed to emit before any consuming callee, so the FV is
passed unconsumed on every call and freed after the first → RC underflow on the
second HTTP request (or second call to any multi-invocation closure).

Root cause: conditions 1–3 of is_borrowed_field had no TPtr exclusion; only
condition 4 guarded against TPtr.  The fix adds a global TPtr guard before all
four conditions.  TPtr sources are always closure structs (\$clo) — their fields
are closure FVs managed by the borrowed' mechanism in insert_rc (d2cf09e).
Marking them as borrowed_field_vars suppressed the required EIncRC.

Regression test: test_perceus_closure_fv_borrowed_clo_incrc verifies the apply
function for a multi-call pipeline closure emits EIncRC for the captured list FV.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(lower): handle PatAtom in pat_tag_and_subs for atom pattern matching

The parser always emits PatAtom for bare atom patterns (:get, :post, …).
pat_tag_and_subs only handled PatLit(LitAtom), which is never produced
by the parser — so every atom branch returned None, was silently dropped
from the pattern matrix, and only the wildcard arm fired in compiled mode.

Fix: add PatAtom cases to pat_tag_and_subs:
  - PatAtom(a, [], _)   → Some(":" ^ a, [])   (bare atom: switch on i64 hash)
  - PatAtom(a, subs, _) → Some(a, subs)        (atom constructor: heap tag)

Add regression test confirming :get/:post branches appear in ECase.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(perceus): suppress spurious EDecRC for locally-owned record fields

Root cause: is_borrowed_field only fired when the source record was in
live_after (borrowed parameter) or _borrowed_field_vars.  For records
returned by cross-module function calls, the source was only in
live_into_e2 — not live_after — so String fields were treated as
independently owned.  post_dec then emitted EDecRC(field) at the last
borrowed-call use, freeing meta.title while the record still held a
reference → dangling pointer and RC underflow.

Fix: is_borrowed_field now also triggers in two new cases:
1. live_before(e2, live_after) includes src — the record is used again
   in the body after this extraction (sequential multi-field access).
2. src is in _var_ctx and is NOT a TPtr — the record is a locally-owned
   heap variable still in scope (e.g. returned by Page.decode).  TPtr
   sources (the closure struct $clo: TPtr TUnit) are excluded so that
   the borrowed' set continues to emit EIncRC for closure FVs before
   consuming calls, which _borrowed_field_vars would otherwise suppress.

Regression test: perceus/local record field no spurious decrc.
1413 → 1414 tests.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(lower): pre-register all DMod names before lowering to prevent type erasure

_ensure_module_lowered re-parses stdlib files on demand using
Module_registry.find_stdlib_file, which returns a RELATIVE path
(e.g. "stdlib/yaml.march").  But the type_map is keyed by spans from
the ABSOLUTE path used by load_stdlib() (e.g. "/Users/.../yaml.march").
The file-path mismatch causes ty_of_span to return TVar "_" for every
expression in the re-parsed module, so all local variables get unknown
types and emit_case falls through to the heap-ADT tag-read path for
Booleans — crashing when the value is actually a tagged integer.

Fix: scan m.mod_decls at the start of lower_module and pre-register
every DMod name in _lowered_modules.  When _ensure_module_lowered fires
for a name already in the combined module, it returns immediately instead
of re-parsing from disk.  Pass 2 then lowers all functions with the
correct type_map.

Reproducer: any .march file calling Yaml.parse_exn / Yaml.skip_ws with
--compile (segfaults before; prints correct output after).
1412 → 1413 tests.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(perceus): prevent RC underflow when tuple param is destructured via ECase in multi-call context

Root cause: field_escape_owns in borrow.ml's owned_in fired for TTuple/TRecord
scrutinees whenever a branch variable escaped via EAtom (returned from the
match arm).  This flipped the tuple parameter to cfg:own in borrow inference,
preventing scrutinee_borrowed from becoming true in Perceus.

With pair:own instead of pair:borrowed, live_after was empty when Perceus
processed the function body, so scrutinee_borrowed = false.
add_scrutinee_free_for then emitted EDecRC(pair) inside the match branch AND
post_dec_vars emitted EDecRC on the extracted String field — without any
matching EIncRC.  On the second call from the loop the field String's RC was
already 0 → "local RC underflow".

Fix: field_escape_owns now returns false for TTuple and TRecord scrutinees.
Perceus's scrutinee_borrowed mechanism already handles field escapes for these
types: when the scrutinee is in live_after (because the param is borrowed),
branch vars are added to live_after and EIncRC is emitted for any extracted
field used at a non-last-use position.  ADT (TCon) scrutinees keep the
existing field_escape_owns logic for FBIP-ownership patterns.

Regression test: perceus/tuple param multi-destruct no RC underflow.
1412 → 1413 tests.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(llvm_emit): don't clear local_names between mutual TCO case bodies

In emit_mutual_tco_group, all case bodies are emitted into the same
LLVM function. Clearing local_names before each case body reset the
alloca uniquification counter, so two case bodies that both declared
a local variable with the same name (e.g. 'st2') each emitted
'%st2.addr = alloca ptr' — a duplicate SSA definition, which clang
rejected with "multiple definition of local value named 'st2.addr'".

Fix: clear local_names once at the start of emit_mutual_tco_group
(matching emit_fn), then clear only var_slot and var_llvm_ty per case
body. This lets the name counter accumulate across all case bodies,
so later case bodies get uniquified names (st2_1, st2_2, …).

Reproducer: any .march file calling Yaml.parse_exn with --compile.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
test(perceus): regression test for List.length+List.nth RC underflow

Adds perceus/list length+nth EIncRC emitted test to verify that
sequential calls List.length(xs) then List.nth(xs, 0) correctly
emit EIncRC(xs) before the first consuming call.

The List.length internal go closure has xs:own because the tail
field $t passes to ECallPtr (conservative-owning), triggering
field_escape_owns = true in borrow inference.  Without the EIncRC,
List.length decrements each Cons node to zero and List.nth reads
freed memory ("local RC underflow").  The fix landed in 0b52510.

1410 → 1412 tests.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
fix(perceus): preserve record liveness when parameter used in multiple sequential calls

Root cause: needs_rc(TRecord _) = false in borrow.ml prevented record params
from entering the borrow-inference fixpoint, so functions that only read a
record via EField were incorrectly marked cfg:own.  Perceus then emitted
dec_rc on the extracted string field at last-use inside the callee; a second
call in the same loop arm read a freed string → "local RC underflow".

Fix (two parts):
1. borrow.ml: needs_rc now returns true for TRecord _ and TTuple _,
   making record/tuple params borrow-eligible.  Functions that only read
   fields are correctly inferred as cfg:borrowed.

2. perceus.ml: new _borrowed_field_vars ref tracks variables extracted from
   a borrowed/live record via EField (and aliases via EAtom).  All four RC
   emission sites — EAtom non-last-use inc, find_inc_vars, post_dec_vars,
   and ELet dead-binding dec — skip variables in this set, preventing the
   callee from emitting any RC ops on field strings owned by the record.

Regression test: perceus/record param multi-call no RC underflow.
1401 → 1410 tests.

author: Matthew Gilliam
message:
feat(show): generic to_string / polymorphic println via Show interface

Show impls for List/Option/Result added to stdlib/prelude.march so that
println([1,2,3]), println(Some(42)), println(Err("oops")) all work without
the caller defining their own Show instance.

Key changes:
- stdlib/prelude.march: impl Show(List(a)), Show(Option(a)),
  Show(Result(a,e)); println(x) delegates to show(x); str(x) alias for
  to_string
- lib/eval/eval.ml: show_dispatch helper routes show/to_string/println
  through impl_tbl; builtin ctors (Ok/Err/Some/None/Cons/Nil) pre-seeded
  in ctor_type_tbl in both reset_scheduler_state and eval_module_env; Show
  impl methods use plain VClosure(env, params, body) so show inside the
  body calls the dispatcher, not itself
- lib/tir/lower.ml: Show$Unit.show synthetic builtin registered
- test/test_march.ml: 7 new tests covering show List, Option Some/None,
  Result Ok/Err, nested list, and polymorphic println typecheck
  (1394 → 1401 tests)
- specs/todos.md + specs/progress.md updated

author: Matthew Gilliam
